one_line_takeaway: ASTRAL chains multimodal LLM reconstruction (from incomplete CPS diagrams), STRIDE-LM/attack-tree generation, and Bayesian-network probabilistic risk scoring into one pipeline, and while ablations show diagrams and semantic guardrails are necessary to keep reconstructed attack paths topologically valid (0.00 impossible paths, 1.00 connectivity throughout), the quality metrics are self-referential and the 14-practitioner evaluation, while positive on usefulness (4.64/5), rated output trustworthiness lowest (4.00/5) and flagged non-determinism and hallucination as open concerns.
key_finding: Ablation (RQ1): Removing diagrams (Text-Only) consistently degraded Valid Trust Boundary counts across all three case studies -- FrostyGoop -23.5%, Medical CPS -14.6%, Solar PV Inverter -43.1% -- confirming diagrams are necessary for topology reconstruction. Removing guardrails (No-Guardrails) reduced Trust Boundaries moderately in FrostyGoop (-11.6%) and MCPS (-10.4%) but more sharply in Solar PV (-27.7%), and qualitatively produced unusable attack models across all artefacts (Graph Connectivity and Impossible Paths could not be computed, shown as '-' in Table 4) even though the quantitative Trust Boundary drop was sometimes modest. Perturbing sampling (High Temperature=0.9, Low top-p=0.1) generally increased raw Trust Boundary counts relative to the Full ASTRAL baseline (up to +47% in FrostyGoop, up to +79.2% in MCPS), which the authors interpret as stochasticity helping the LLM surface latent architectural elements rather than as an unambiguous quality improvement. Across every guardrails-on variant and case study, Graph Connectivity stayed at a perfect 1.00 +/- 0.00 and Impossible Attack Paths stayed at 0.00 +/- 0.00, i.e., the pipeline never generated a topologically invalid attack path in these experiments when guardrails were active. Practitioner validation (RQ2-RQ4): across 14 participants, average Likert ratings (1-5) were Usefulness 4.64, Ease of use/navigation 4.29, Likelihood of future use/recommendation 4.36, and Trustworthiness/reliability of outputs 4.00 (the lowest-rated criterion). Qualitative feedback praised rapid structured extraction of components/trust boundaries/attack entry points from diagrams, but flagged non-deterministic outputs across runs, hallucination risk, latency during attack-path generation, limited transparency/traceability of how threats/risk scores were derived, no human-in-the-loop parameter tuning, and no direct mapping of threats onto the architecture diagram.
core_contribution: The paper introduces ASTRAL (Architecture-Centric Security Threat Risk Assessment using LLMs), a three-phase pipeline for cyber-physical system (CPS) security assessment that starts from incomplete or outdated architectural documentation. Phase 1 uses a multimodal LLM (image-based artefacts such as data flow diagrams plus text) with prompt chaining, few-shot examples, and semantic guardrails (grounded in IEC 62443 zone mappings, the Purdue Enterprise Reference Architecture, and AutomationML role classes) to reconstruct a structured 'architectural narration' from fragmented diagrams and documentation. Phase 2 feeds this narration into STRIDE-LM-based threat elicitation and attack-tree/attack-path generation via structured JSON-output prompts. Phase 3 synthesizes the reconstructed architecture and threat/attack model into a Bayesian Network (encoded in AutomationML/IEC 62714 format) that is parameterised from CVSS/EPSS-derived exposure probabilities and solved via variable elimination to produce posterior probabilities of successful attack, severe impact, a composite risk score, and a system availability score. The authors built a prototype tool implementing this workflow and evaluated it with (a) an ablation study across three CPS case studies and (b) a mixed-methods survey/demo study with 14 cybersecurity practitioners.
threat_model: The paper does not assume a fixed adversary model of its own system (ASTRAL is a defensive/analyst-facing tool, not a system under attack). Instead, within its outputs it models CPS attackers generically as external/internal entities pursuing objectives such as power disruption, asset damage, persistent foothold in isolated OT environments, or bypassing safety controls, and generates STRIDE-LM (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege, Lateral Movement) threat scenarios and attack trees rooted at a single attacker node connected to all leaf nodes of the attack paths. The running example is the real-world FrostyGoop attack (OT malware using Modbus TCP that caused a two-day heating outage affecting over 600 apartment buildings in Ukraine, discovered 2024), used as a case study rather than as the paper's original threat research.
limitations: The paper's own stated/implied limitations: (1) The ablation study's four reconstruction-quality metrics are all 'no-ground-truth' / self-referential (LLM-as-a-judge for coherence, structural counts against the same model's own output) rather than validated against an expert-labelled ground-truth architecture, so 'quality' gains from perturbed sampling (e.g., +79.2% Trust Boundaries under Low top-p in MCPS) are not independently verified as correct rather than merely more numerous. (2) The 14 practitioners were recruited from the first author's professional network (a convenience sample), which the paper itself acknowledges as a selection-bias limitation, and the evaluation is explicitly framed as 'exploratory' rather than a controlled comparative study against a baseline (non-LLM) assessment workflow. (3) Non-determinism and hallucination risk are inherent and acknowledged: participants reported inconsistent outputs across runs and unverifiable/spurious content, and trustworthiness/reliability was the lowest-rated survey dimension (4.00 vs. 4.64 for usefulness). (4) The current prototype implements reconstruction using only image-based artefacts, even though the framework is described as 'likely extensible' to configuration files, asset inventories, and live operational data -- those other data sources are not implemented or evaluated. (5) The two additional case studies (Medical CPS, Solar PV Inverter) are presented as illustrative/sample analyses rather than validated against independently confirmed ground-truth architectures or incidents in the way FrostyGoop (a documented real-world attack) is. (6) Practitioners flagged concrete usability/latency and transparency gaps: delays during attack-path generation on complex architectures, information overload, suboptimal visualization, absence of human-in-the-loop override of impact/exploitability assumptions, and no direct overlay of threats onto the architecture diagram.

datasets_used: FrostyGoop attack scenario (real-world 2024 OT malware incident affecting municipal heating in Ukraine), reconstructed from an incomplete data flow diagram (DFD) built with the Microsoft Threat Modelling Tool from open-source intelligence
Medical Cyber-Physical System (MCPS) case study using a high-level schematic diagram (body-area sensors, SDN controllers/gateways, cloud medical data platforms, EHR systems)

Solar PV Inverter case study using a Bayesian Network graph derived from partial site diagrams, inverter-network schematics, and vendor advisories referencing the SUN:DOWN study on solar PV inverter vulnerabilities
quantitative_results: FrostyGoop Full ASTRAL ablation baseline: Trust Boundaries 5.10 +/- 1.29, Connectivity 1.00 +/- 0.00, Impossible Paths 0.00 +/- 0.00, Semantic Coherence 4.85 +/- 0.05 (n=10 runs)
FrostyGoop Text-Only variant: Trust Boundaries drop to 3.90 (-23.5% vs baseline), Coherence 4.58 (both significant at p<=0.10)
FrostyGoop High Temperature variant: Trust Boundaries rise to 7.50 (+47% vs baseline)
Medical CPS Low top-p variant: Trust Boundaries rise to 8.60 (+79.2% vs baseline, the largest ablation effect reported)
Solar PV Inverter Text-Only variant: Trust Boundaries drop to 3.70 (-43.1% vs baseline, the largest degradation reported)

FrostyGoop BN sample results (Table 1): posterior P(successful attack)=0.3404, P(severe impact)=0.6204, composite Risk Score=21.12%, System Availability=25.04%; example vulnerability node V05 'Exploit Weak HMI Authentication or Session Hijacking' with base P(Exposure)=0.47, calibrated P(Exposure) mean=0.4982 (95% CI 0.4032-0.5933)

Medical CPS sample results (Table 2): Risk Score 39.15%, System Availability 14.01%; example vulnerability V01 'Wind River VxWorks Buffer Overflow' (CVE-2019-12255) with P(Exposure)=0.9, P(Severe Impact)=0.85

Solar PV Inverter sample results (Table 2): Risk Score 4.95%, System Availability 38.65%; example vulnerability V05 'Physical Access to Inverter Configuration Ports' (no CVE) with base P(Exposure)=0.11, calibrated P(Exposure) mean=0.4771 (95% CI 0.3820-0.5721)
Practitioner survey (n=14, Table 5, 1-5 Likert): Usefulness 4.64, Ease of use/navigation 4.29, Likelihood of future use/recommendation 4.36, Trustworthiness and reliability of outputs 4.00
baselines_compared: No external/prior-tool baseline is used; comparisons are internal ablations against a 'Full ASTRAL' configuration (multimodal input + guardrails enabled + temperature=0.1 + top_p=0.9), with variants: Text-Only (diagrams removed), No-Guardrails (semantic guardrails disabled), High Temperature (temperature=0.9), and Low top-p (top_p=0.1), each evaluated with Wilcoxon signed-rank tests at p<=0.10 against the Full ASTRAL baseline
